Extending McFarland’s cross-trial decomposition to non-uniform trials and non-homogeneous stimuli

A methodological note

fem-v1-fovea methods note

2026-05-29

Summary

McFarland, Cumming & Butts (2016) introduced a cross-trial, eye-position-conditioned decomposition that separates the stimulus-driven and stimulus-independent components of V1 responses in the presence of fixational eye movements (FEMs). It is now the standard estimator for the FEM fraction 1−α1-\alpha, the corrected noise covariance CnoisecorrC_{\text{noise}}^{\text{corr}}, and the Fano factor under FEMs. Two assumptions sit inside it:

  • (A1) Uniform trial/time-bin structure — every analysis time bin tt has the same number of trials ntn_t, so the across-time-bin weighting is the same for every term in the law-of-total-(co)variance (LOTC).
  • (A2) Statistically stationary stimulus — the across-time-bin distribution of r(t,e)r(t,e) at a fixed eye position is the same at every ee (equivalently, 𝔼t[rk(t,e)]\mathbb{E}_t[r^k(t,e)] is independent of ee for the moments used). It then does not matter which eye-position distribution the rate moments are averaged over, and the close-pair restriction in (7) is free.

Both assumptions fail in the structured fixRSVP stimulus used here. Fixation durations vary across trials, so the per-time-bin trial count ntn_t drops across time bins; and the windowed natural-image stimulus is far from translation-invariant, so the rate r(t,e)r(t,e) depends on absolute eye position. This note develops, validates, and discusses a methodological extension of McFarland et al. for each assumption violation:

  1. Consistent time-bin weighting under variable ntn_t. The close-pair rate estimator is intrinsically pair-count weighted (∝nt(nt−1)/2\propto n_t(n_t{-}1)/2) across time bins, while McFarland’s literal PSTH term is uniform (1/T1/T) and the mean is trial-count weighted. Under variable ntn_t this mix biases the cross-cell covariance — the corrected noise correlation is pushed negative and the shuffle-null control deviates from zero — while the per-cell 1−α1-\alpha is barely affected. The bias is additive, not multiplicative in origin (a gain envelope is weighting-invariant; a random per-cell onset transient is not), and is removed by pinning every term to one weighting. The two consistent choices (ntn_t and 1/T1/T) are equivalent in the model; we default to ntn_t for its lower finite-sample variance. Demonstrated on synthetic ground truth and on 25 real fixRSVP sessions.
  2. Eye-position-distribution matching under a non-homogeneous stimulus. Close pairs at threshold Δe<ε\Delta e<\varepsilon are sampled in proportion to the squared eye-position density p(e)2p(e)^2, while the total covariance and the PSTH covariance are over p(e)p(e). We restore consistency with an importance-reweighted estimator that has the target eye distribution as a parameter, with two principled choices (pp and p2p^2). Their gap measures whether the rate has spatial structure on the fixation scale — non-zero even under (A2) when ℓ∼σe\ell\sim\sigma_e.

Sections 1 and 2 set up McFarland’s estimator and the synthetic model that breaks both assumptions. Sections 3 and 4 develop and validate one extension each, on synthetic ground truth and on real fixRSVP data. A companion implementation note (note_pipeline.md) records the production-pipeline state — Extension 1 has already been integrated; Extension 2 is implemented in this folder and gated.

1. Background: the cross-trial decomposition under FEMs

1.1 The law of total (co)variance

Let Yci(t)Y_c^i(t) be the spike count of neuron cc on trial ii at analysis time bin tt (a frozen-stimulus time bin). Each trial samples an absolute eye position ee from the fixational distribution p(e)p(e); we treat ee as approximately constant over the short counting window and approximately independent of tt. Writing the firing rate as rc(t,e)=𝔼[Yc∣t,e]r_c(t,e) = \mathbb{E}[Y_c \mid t, e], the law of total variance partitions the stimulus-driven rate variance into a stimulus-locked (PSTH) part and an eye-movement (FEM) part:

Vart,e∼p[rc(t,e)]⏟total rate variance=Vart[𝔼e∼p[rc∣t]]⏟PSTH variance+𝔼t[Vare∼p[rc∣t]]⏟FEM variance. \underbrace{\mathrm{Var}_{t,\,e\sim p}\!\big[r_c(t,e)\big]}_{\text{total rate variance}} \;=\; \underbrace{\mathrm{Var}_t\!\Big[\,\mathbb{E}_{e\sim p}\!\big[r_c \mid t\big]\Big]}_{\text{PSTH variance}} \;+\; \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_t\!\Big[\,\mathrm{Var}_{e\sim p}\!\big[r_c \mid t\big]\Big]}_{\text{FEM variance}} .

(1)

McFarland et al. define the fraction of stimulus-driven variance captured by the PSTH,

αc=Vart[𝔼e∼p[rc∣t]]Vart,e∼p[rc],1−αc=FEM fraction. \alpha_c \;=\; \frac{\mathrm{Var}_t\big[\mathbb{E}_{e\sim p}[r_c\mid t]\big]} {\mathrm{Var}_{t,e\sim p}[r_c]} , \qquad 1-\alpha_c \;=\; \text{FEM fraction}.

(2)

The pairwise analogue partitions the count covariance, Covt,e∼p[Ym,Yn]=Covt,e∼p[rm,rn]+𝔼[noise cov]\mathrm{Cov}_{t,e\sim p}[Y_m,Y_n] = \mathrm{Cov}_{t,e\sim p}[r_m,r_n] + \mathbb{E}\big[\text{noise cov}\big], so the FEM-corrected (stimulus-independent) covariance is

Cnoisecorr=Ctotal−Crate,Fanoc=(Cnoisecorr)ccr‾c, C_{\text{noise}}^{\text{corr}} \;=\; C_{\text{total}} \;-\; C_{\text{rate}}, \qquad \text{Fano}_c \;=\; \frac{\big(C_{\text{noise}}^{\text{corr}}\big)_{cc}}{\bar r_c},

(3)

where Ctotal=Covt,e∼p[Y]C_{\text{total}} = \mathrm{Cov}_{t,e\sim p}[Y] is the raw count covariance and Crate=Covt,e∼p[r]C_{\text{rate}} = \mathrm{Cov}_{t,e\sim p}[r] is the total rate covariance over the same distribution pp. Equation (3) is only meaningful if CrateC_{\text{rate}} and CtotalC_{\text{total}} are measured over one and the same eye-position distribution; this is the consistency requirement that the rest of this note is about.

1.2 The cross-trial trick: distinct trials cancel the observation noise

We never observe rc(t,e)r_c(t,e) directly — only the noisy counts YcY_c. The engine of the whole method is that the observation noise is independent across distinct trials, so for two different trials i≠ji\neq j at the same time bin tt,

𝔼[Yci(t)Ycj(t)|ei,ej]=rc(t,ei)rc(t,ej). \mathbb{E}\big[Y_c^i(t)\,Y_c^j(t)\,\big|\,e_i,e_j\big] = r_c(t,e_i)\,r_c(t,e_j).

(4)

The product of two distinct trials’ counts is an unbiased estimate of the product of their underlying rates, with the Poisson/observation variance removed. Every estimator below is a different way of averaging these distinct-trial products — and the choice of which pairs to average sets the eye-position distribution.

1.3 Two families of estimator

Signal (PSTH) variance and covariance — all distinct pairs. Averaging (4) over all distinct pairs at the same time bin, then over time bins, estimates the PSTH (stimulus-locked) second moment (McFarland’s Eq. 6):

Vart[𝔼irci(t)]̂=⟨⟨Yci(t)Ycj(t)⟩i≠j⟩t−Y‾c2, \widehat{\mathrm{Var}_t\!\big[\mathbb E_i\, r^i_c(t)\big]} = \big\langle \langle Y_c^i(t)\,Y_c^j(t)\rangle_{i\ne j}\big\rangle_t - \bar Y_c^{\,2},

(5)

Covt[𝔼irmi,𝔼irni]̂=⟨⟨Ymi(t)Ynj(t)⟩i≠j⟩t−Y‾mY‾n. \widehat{\mathrm{Cov}_t\!\big[\mathbb E_i\, r^i_m,\ \mathbb E_i\, r^i_n\big]} = \big\langle \langle Y_m^i(t)\,Y_n^j(t)\rangle_{i\ne j}\big\rangle_t - \bar Y_m\,\bar Y_n.

(6)

Because all same-time-bin pairs are used, the per-bin mean averages over the full fixational distribution p(e)p(e): these terms live on p(e)p(e).

Eye-conditioned rate variance and covariance — close pairs only. Restrict the same averages to pairs whose eye trajectories nearly coincide, Δeij<ε\Delta e_{ij}<\varepsilon. This drives ei≈ej≈ee_i\approx e_j\approx e, so (4) →rc(t,e)2\to r_c(t,e)^2: the total rate second moment with the Poisson noise removed (their Eqs. 8 and 16),

Vari,t[rc]̂=⟨⟨Yci(t)Ycj(t)∣Δeij<ε⟩i≠j⟩t−Y‾c2, \widehat{\mathrm{Var}_{i,t}[r_c]} = \big\langle \langle Y_c^i(t)\,Y_c^j(t)\mid \Delta e_{ij}<\varepsilon\rangle_{i\ne j}\big\rangle_t - \bar Y_c^{\,2},

(7)

Covi,t[rm,rn]̂=⟨⟨Ymi(t)Ynj(t)∣Δeij<ε⟩i≠j⟩t−Y‾mY‾n. \widehat{\mathrm{Cov}_{i,t}[r_m,r_n]} = \big\langle \langle Y_m^i(t)\,Y_n^j(t)\mid \Delta e_{ij}<\varepsilon\rangle_{i\ne j}\big\rangle_t - \bar Y_m\,\bar Y_n.

(8)

The close-pair restriction is exactly what cancels the Poisson noise across distinct trials — but, as §4 shows, it also silently changes the eye-position distribution these terms live on.

1.4 The reported quantities

McFarland’s reported quantities combine the two estimator families. The FEM fraction is αc=(5)/(7)\alpha_c = (5)/(7) (Eq. 10); the corrected noise covariance and Fano factor are Cnoisecorr=Ctotal−CrateC_{\text{noise}}^{\text{corr}} = C_{\text{total}} - C_{\text{rate}} from (3) with the close-pair CrateC_{\text{rate}} from (8). McFarland et al. also give an analytical form for α\alpha via the Fourier transform of pp (their Eq. 9), in which α\alpha weights the rate spectrum |R(k)|2|R(k)|^2 by the eye-distribution spectrum |P(k)|2|P(k)|^2.

1.5 Three estimators on two weighting axes

Each estimator above averages distinct-trial products in a different way. Reading directly off (5)–(8) and the LOTC LHS of (1), each lives on an implicit weighting in two dimensions — across analysis time bins (wtw_t) and across eye positions (qq):

Estimator implicit wtw_t (across-bin) implicit qq (across-eye)
CtotalC_\text{total} — LHS of (1); sample variance over all (i,t)(i,t) ∝nt\propto n_t (trial-count) pp
CpsthC_\text{psth} — Eq. (6): ⟨⟨YiYj⟩i≠j⟩t−Y‾Y‾⊤\langle\langle Y^i Y^j\rangle_{i\ne j}\rangle_t - \bar Y\bar Y^\top 1/T1/T for the ⟨⟨YiYj⟩⟩\langle\langle Y^iY^j\rangle\rangle term; ∝nt\propto n_t for Y‾Y‾⊤\bar Y\bar Y^\top pp
CrateC_\text{rate} — Eq. (8): ⟨⟨YiYj∣Δe<ε⟩i≠j⟩t−Y‾Y‾⊤\langle\langle Y^i Y^j \mid \Delta e<\varepsilon\rangle_{i\ne j}\rangle_t - \bar Y\bar Y^\top 1/T1/T for the ⟨⟨YiYj⟩⟩\langle\langle Y^iY^j\rangle\rangle term; ∝nt\propto n_t for Y‾Y‾⊤\bar Y\bar Y^\top p2p^2 for the ⟨⟨YiYj⟩⟩\langle\langle Y^iY^j\rangle\rangle term; pp for Y‾Y‾⊤\bar Y\bar Y^\top

The notation in the cells:

  • wt=1/Tw_t = 1/T (“uniform across bins”). Bin tt contributes 1/T1/T regardless of ntn_t — compute a per-bin mean first, then average the per-bin means uniformly across TT bins. This is McFarland’s literal reading of the nested bracket ⟨⟨⋅⟩i≠j⟩t\langle\langle\,\cdot\,\rangle_{i\ne j}\rangle_t in (6) and (8): the inner ⟨⋅⟩i≠j\langle\cdot\rangle_{i\ne j} averages within bin tt, the outer ⟨⋅⟩t\langle\cdot\rangle_t averages bins uniformly.
  • wt∝ntw_t \propto n_t (“trial-count”). Bin tt contributes weight proportional to its trial count — pool all (i,t)(i, t) samples and weight each equally; bin tt then appears ntn_t times in the pool and contributes ntn_t units of weight. This is how the sample variance of YY pooled over (i,t)(i,t) is computed (so CtotalC_\text{total} lives here), and how the global mean Y‾=1∑tnt∑i,tYi(t)\bar Y = \frac{1}{\sum_t n_t}\sum_{i,t} Y^i(t) lives (so the Y‾Y‾⊤\bar Y\bar Y^\top subtractor lives here).

Concretely: under the fixRSVP staircase ntn_t ranging 15→36015 \to 360, a 360-trial bin and a 15-trial bin contribute equally under wt=1/Tw_t = 1/T, but the 360-trial bin contributes 24×24\times more weight under wt∝ntw_t \propto n_t. The two weightings coincide only when ntn_t is constant — then 1/T1/T and nt/∑ntn_t / \sum n_t are the same number for every bin.

The across-eye axis qq has only two values in the table, and they live on different cells for a concrete sampling reason:

  • q=pq = p (“full fixational density”). The across-eye distribution is the actual viewing distribution p(e)p(e) each trial samples its eye position from. Any average that uses all same-time-bin trials averages the rate over pp: the global mean Y‾\bar Y, CtotalC_\text{total}, and the all-pairs PSTH second moment (6) all live at q=pq = p.
  • q=p2q = p^2 (“close-pair density”). The close-pair restriction Δeij<ε\Delta e_{ij} < \varepsilon in (7)–(8) keeps only pairs whose two eye positions nearly coincide. For ei,eje_i, e_j drawn independently from pp, the probability that both land in a small ball around ee is ∝p(e)⋅p(e)=p(e)2\propto p(e)\cdot p(e) = p(e)^2, so the close-pair second moment averages r(t,e)2r(t,e)^2 over the squared density p2(e)≡p(e)2/∫p(e′)2de′p^2(e) \equiv p(e)^2 / \int p(e')^2\,de', not over pp. For an isotropic Gaussian p=𝒩(0,σe2I)p = \mathcal N(0,\sigma_e^2 I) this is exactly 𝒩(0,σe22I)\mathcal N(0,\tfrac{\sigma_e^2}{2} I) — a tighter, more central Gaussian with half the variance. This single fact (the homogeneity caveat McFarland’s stationary stimulus lets him sidestep) is the entire origin of the qq-axis mismatch, and §4 is built on it.

Throughout, a superscript on α\alpha names the across-eye distribution the ratio (2) is evaluated over: αp\alpha^p (and 1−αp1-\alpha^p) is McFarland’s quantity under the actual viewing density pp, while αp2\alpha^{p^2} is the same ratio evaluated under the close-pair density p2p^2. The two differ whenever the rate’s second moment depends on absolute eye position (the (A2) failure of §4); they coincide under McFarland’s stationary-stimulus assumption.

The cross-trial second-moment term ⟨⟨YiYj⟩⟩\langle\langle Y^iY^j\rangle\rangle — the part that averages distinct-trial products — is distinct from the Y‾Y‾⊤\bar Y\bar Y^\top subtractor because the two are computed from the data differently (close-pair products at fixed tt vs the global mean over all (i,t)(i,t)), so they live on different (wt,q)(w_t, q) cells.

The LOTC (1) holds term-by-term only when all three estimators land on a single (wt,q)(w_t, q). The literal McFarland forms do not: even inside one estimator the second-moment term and the Y‾Y‾⊤\bar Y\bar Y^\top subtractor disagree on wtw_t (second-moment term at 1/T1/T, subtractor at ∝nt\propto n_t). And CrateC_\text{rate}’s second-moment term is the only cell at q=p2q = p^2 (the close-pair conditional density, §4.1); every other cell is at q=pq = p.

Two assumptions in McFarland’s regime make these inconsistencies invisible:

  • (A1) Uniform trial/time-bin structure. When ntn_t is constant, ∝nt\propto n_t and 1/T1/T coincide up to a global scaling, so the wtw_t column collapses to a single value across all three estimators (and across the second-moment / Y‾\bar Y split within each).

  • (A2) Statistically stationary stimulus. The across-time-bin distribution of r(t,e)r(t,e) at a fixed eye position is the same at every ee, i.e. 𝔼t[rk(t,e)]\mathbb{E}_t[r^k(t,e)] is independent of ee for the moments used. Then 𝔼e∼D[𝔼t[rk(t,e)]]\mathbb{E}_{e\sim D}\!\big[\mathbb E_t[r^k(t,e)]\big] is the same for every distribution DD, so the qq column collapses: the close-pair restriction in (7)–(8) — which silently sets D=p2D = p^2 — gives the same answer as the actual viewing target D=pD = p. McFarland et al. state the assumption at the level of the stimulus (their text around Eqs. M7–M10):

    “by restricting analysis to trial pairs where Δeij≈0\Delta e_{ij}\approx 0, [the estimator] gives an estimate of 𝔼[r2(e,t)]\mathbb{E}[r^2(e,t)] under the conditional eye position distribution p(e∣Δe≈0)p(e\mid\Delta e\approx 0) rather than p(e)p(e). However, for a stimulus that is statistically invariant to spatial translations (such as used in our study), the expectation of r2(e,t)r^2(e,t) with respect to any distribution over ee will be the same.”

    Stimulus stationarity is sufficient: when each frame is a sample from a translation-invariant random field, the rate distribution at one eye position is, in distribution, the rate distribution at any other, so the across-time-bin moments at fixed ee do not depend on ee. McFarland’s ternary bar noise has this property; the structured fixRSVP images used here do not.

In McFarland’s regime — (A1) ∩ (A2) — every cell of the table collapses to a single (wt,q)(w_t, q), the three estimators agree, and (1) reduces to the textbook LOTC. Outside that regime each axis fails on its own and the LOTC fails term-by-term. §3 develops two consistent choices on the wtw_t axis under (A1) failure; §4 develops two consistent choices on the qq axis under (A2) failure. The two extensions are orthogonal — each fills one column of the table — and §2 makes both failures concrete on the fixRSVP stimulus.

2. Our setting and the synthetic model

Having laid out the two assumption failures theoretically, we now build intuition for exactly how they manifest, using an analytical model with a closed-form solution for the PSTH and rate variance. The model lets us turn each assumption on or off as a switch and read off the consequences against ground truth — first describing where the fixRSVP setting breaks each assumption (§2.1, §2.2), then the generator itself (§2.3), and finally a confirmation that McFarland’s estimator is exact when both assumptions hold (§2.4).

2.1 Violation of (A1): uneven fixation durations ⇒\Rightarrow variable ntn_t

fixRSVP trials are organized around fixational fixation periods of variable duration. Aligned to fixation onset and binned into analysis time bins, this gives a monotonically decreasing per-time-bin trial count: every trial is fixating in early bins, but only long-fixation trials contribute to late bins. On a representative session (Allen 2022-04-13, 49 cells) ntn_t ranged from 42 to 78 across time bins (CV ≈0.19\approx 0.19); on our broader set of cells the spread is larger. Under this ntn_t variation, the close-pair rate estimator weights each time bin by nt(nt−1)/2n_t(n_t{-}1)/2 pairs while a uniform split-half PSTH estimator weights each time bin by 1/T1/T — these only coincide if ntn_t is constant.

Variable fixation duration is the dominant cause of differing ntn_t here, but it is not the only one: anything that masks out individual (trial, time-bin) samples produces the same effect. Varying neuron stability (a unit drifts in or out of isolation over a session), or any quality gate applied per sample — most commonly excluding samples whose eye position falls outside predefined bounds — also leaves a time-bin-dependent count of valid samples. Extension 1 is therefore the general fix for an arbitrarily masked sample array, of which the variable-duration staircase is one instance; the synthetic below uses the staircase as the cleanest controllable case.

2.2 Violation of (A2): the windowed fixRSVP stimulus

fixRSVP presents a natural image at a fixed location on the screen, behind a spatial window (aperture). The neuron’s receptive field (RF) is fixed in retinotopic coordinates, so in screen coordinates it moves with the eye. Two mechanisms make the across-time-bin distribution of rc(t,e)r_c(t,e) at fixed eye position depend on absolute eye position — directly violating (A2):

  1. Windowing — a hard non-stationarity. Once the fixation offset is large enough to carry the RF off the windowed image, the RF samples only the uniform gray background and the rate collapses to the gray-screen baseline regardless of analysis time bin tt. So at peripheral ee the across-time-bin distribution of rr is concentrated at baseline; at central ee it is strongly stimulus-modulated. The across-time-bin moments at fixed ee are therefore strongly ee-dependent — before any image content is considered.
  2. Image structure. Within the window, drift slides the structured image across the RF, so which feature drives the cell — and how strongly — depends on absolute eye position. The across-time-bin distribution of rr at each ee samples a different patch of a fixed image, not different draws from a translation-invariant ensemble; the per-ee moments differ accordingly.

2.3 A unified generative model that breaks both assumptions

We validate every estimator claim below against a single synthetic generator (synthetic.py) chosen so that (A1) and (A2) are switches on top of one architecture. With both switches off, the generator sits squarely in McFarland’s regime, with a closed-form 1−αp1-\alpha^p that depends on a single ratio — the rate’s spatial scale relative to the fixation scale — and covers (0,1)(0,1) as that ratio is swept.

Rate field. For neuron cc, analysis time bin tt, and absolute eye position e∈ℝ2e\in\mathbb R^2,

rc(t,e)=μ0+Mc(e)α(t)st(e), r_c(t,e) \;=\; \mu_0 \;+\; M_c(e)\,\alpha(t)\,s_t(e),

(9)

with three separable ingredients:

  • st(⋅)s_t(\cdot) — a per-time-bin i.i.d. draw of a stationary 2-D zero-mean Gaussian random field with covariance K(δ)=τ2exp(−∥δ∥2/(2ℓ2))K(\delta) = \tau^2\exp\!\big(-\lVert\delta\rVert^2/(2\ell^2)\big). This is the rate map at time bin tt. At any fixed ee, st(e)∼𝒩(0,τ2)s_t(e)\sim\mathcal N(0,\tau^2) across time bins — the across-time-bin distribution is independent of ee, so the field component is (A2) by construction.
  • α(t)\alpha(t) — a per-time-bin multiplicative amplitude (gain) envelope. Default α≡1\alpha\equiv 1. When the synthetic targets Extension 1 (§3) we set α(t)\alpha(t) to decay across time bins, co-varying with ntn_t, so the two consistent wtw_t directions differ in finite-sample variance (§3.4). The gain envelope does not by itself bias the cross-cell covariance — it is weighting-invariant (§3.3); that bias requires an additive onset transient, introduced in §3.3 as a separate illustrative component on top of α(t)\alpha(t).
  • Mc(e)M_c(e) — the per-cell spatial mask in [0,1][0,1], the (A2) switch. Physically, Mc(e)M_c(e) is the fraction of the windowed stimulus the cell sees at eye position ee: when the RF leaves the window, M(e)→0M(e)\to 0 and the rate collapses to baseline regardless of time bin, which is precisely §2.2’s “hard windowing” mechanism. M≡1M\equiv 1 recovers a fully homogeneous stimulus; any non-constant MM breaks (A2) at the second moment: 𝔼t[r2(t,e)]=μ02+M(e)2𝔼t[α2]τ2\mathbb E_t[r^2(t,e)]=\mu_0^2 + M(e)^2\,\mathbb E_t[\alpha^2]\,\tau^2, which depends on ee through M(e)2M(e)^2 — the exact form McFarland’s caveat is about (their text around Eqs. M7–M10). The first moment 𝔼t[r(t,e)]=μ0\mathbb E_t[r(t,e)] = \mu_0 stays ee-independent.

Three mask shapes span the relevant cases:

mask kind Mc(e)M_c(e) physical role
flat 11 homogeneous stimulus — (A2) holds
central exp(−∥e∥2/(2σM2))\exp\!\big(-\lVert e\rVert^2/(2\sigma_M^2)\big) windowed stimulus: cell sees the stimulus near fixation, only baseline in periphery
eccentric 1−exp(−∥e∥2/(2σM2))1 - \exp\!\big(-\lVert e\rVert^2/(2\sigma_M^2)\big) the bounded complement — cell suppressed at fixation, sees the stimulus when eye drifts

central is the workhorse stand-in for the windowed fixRSVP mechanism. flat is the regime in which McFarland’s estimator is unbiased; we use it in §2.4 to sanity-check the estimator against a non-trivial analytical 1−αp1-\alpha^p. eccentric stress-tests the corrected estimator on a mask whose variance lives where close pairs are rare or biased.

Eye distribution. Eyes are drawn i.i.d. e∼p=𝒩(0,σe2I)e\sim p=\mathcal N(0,\sigma_e^2 I) per (trial, time-bin), σe=0.15∘\sigma_e=0.15^\circ (realistic fixational drift). For an isotropic Gaussian pp, the close-pair density p(e)2p(e)^2 is exactly 𝒩(0,σe2/2I)\mathcal N(0,\sigma_e^2/2\,I) — a tighter Gaussian with half the variance — so the LOTC under pp or p2p^2 has a closed form (Appendix §A.1).

Trial structure. The (trial, time-bin) array has shape (Ntr,T)(N_{\text{tr}}, T). With ntr/bin=Nonen_{\text{tr/bin}}=\text{None} every time bin has NtrN_{\text{tr}} trials, recovering McFarland’s uniform-trial regime. To violate (A1) we set ntr/bin=(nt)t=1Tn_{\text{tr/bin}}=(n_t)_{t=1}^{T} — a monotonically decaying staircase (default lo 1515, hi ∼Ntr\sim N_{\text{tr}}, see Fig. 1A) that mimics the fixation-duration distribution. Entries beyond ntn_t in each time bin are masked invalid.

Observation noise. Spikes are drawn Yc∼Poisson(rc(t,e))Y_c \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(r_c(t,e)) (optionally with a shared latent giving a known stimulus-independent noise covariance). The rate is marginally 𝒩(μ0,M(e)2τ2)\mathcal N(\mu_0, M(e)^2\tau^2); with μ0=6,τ≤1\mu_0=6,\,\tau\leq 1, Pr[r<0]∼10−9\Pr[r<0]\sim 10^{-9} and the clip to r≥10−6r\geq 10^{-6} effectively never triggers. An exponential link is the standard alternative but obscures the closed form.

Stimulus-frame caveat. The synthetic treats each analysis time bin tt as an independent fresh draw of the field sts_t, mirroring McFarland’s “stimulus phase” abstraction. In real fixRSVP this is an idealization: the natural-image stimulus refreshes at 2020 Hz while the analysis typically operates at 6060 or 120120 Hz, so several consecutive analysis time bins fall within the same 5050 ms stimulus-frame interval and share the same underlying sts_t — they are highly correlated, not i.i.d. Across stimulus-frame boundaries the rate is genuinely refreshed. The practical consequence (developed in §A.9) is that the effective TT controlling the across-bin SEM floor is closer to #fixations×#stim-frames per fixation\#\text{fixations}\times\#\text{stim-frames per fixation} than the raw bin count #fixations×#bins per fixation\#\text{fixations}\times\#\text{bins per fixation}. Within- stimulus-frame reliability — how the cell’s response varies across the several analysis bins of one frame — is a future direction; the analyses below treat this as out-of-scope and use the i.i.d.-bin synthetic as a clean reference for the McFarland-style estimator.

Closed-form ground truth. With MM depending only on ee and α\alpha only on tt, and the field zero-mean, the LOTC decomposition admits a closed form under any distribution DD over ee and any time-bin weighting wtw_t (derivation in Appendix §A.1):

VartotalD,w=𝔼w[α2]τ2𝔼D[M2],VarpsthD,w=𝔼w[α2]IM,K,D, \mathrm{Var}_{\text{total}}^{D,w} = \mathbb E_w[\alpha^2]\,\tau^2\,\mathbb E_D[M^2], \qquad \mathrm{Var}_{\text{psth}}^{D,w} = \mathbb E_w[\alpha^2]\,I_{M,K,D},

(10)

with IM,K,D=∬M(e1)M(e2)K(e1−e2)D(e1)D(e2)de1de2I_{M,K,D}=\iint M(e_1) M(e_2)\,K(e_1{-}e_2)\,D(e_1)\,D(e_2)\,de_1\,de_2. The ratio 1−αD,w=1−IM,K,D/(τ2𝔼D[M2])1-\alpha^{D,w} = 1 - I_{M,K,D}/(\tau^2\,\mathbb E_D[M^2]) is invariant under time-bin weighting: the envelope factor cancels. Both wtw_t directions of Extension 1 therefore target the same truth on the ratio; they differ only in finite-sample efficiency (§3.4).

For the flat mask and Gaussian D,KD, K, the integral closes analytically:

1−αp=2σe2ℓ2+2σe2,1−αp2=σe2ℓ2+σe2, 1-\alpha^p = \frac{2\sigma_e^2}{\ell^2 + 2\sigma_e^2}, \qquad 1-\alpha^{p^2} = \frac{\sigma_e^2}{\ell^2 + \sigma_e^2},

(11)

a single-parameter family in ℓ/σe\ell/\sigma_e that covers (0,1)(0,1). The central (Gaussian) and eccentric (one-minus-Gaussian) masks close in the same way (Appendix §A.3, §A.4); all three closed forms were checked against Monte Carlo (sampling noise ≲10−3\lesssim 10^{-3}).

These closed forms already expose the heart of Extension 2 (§4), before any estimator is introduced: the decomposition depends on which eye-position distribution it is evaluated over. Even for the homogeneous flat mask, 1−αp1-\alpha^p and 1−αp21-\alpha^{p^2} differ at every finite ℓ\ell (Fig. 0a A) — the close-pair density p2p^2 is tighter than the viewing density pp, and a rate map with spatial structure on the fixation scale (ℓ∼σe\ell\sim\sigma_e) integrates to a different variance under each. Their gap peaks at ℓ/σe≈1.18\ell/\sigma_e\approx 1.18 and vanishes only in the two degenerate limits (ℓ→0\ell\to 0, fully decorrelated rates; ℓ→∞\ell\to\infty, a spatially uniform field). For a non-homogeneous mask the dependence is amplified, and its size is set jointly by the mask width σM\sigma_M and ℓ/σe\ell/\sigma_e: Fig. 0a B sweeps ℓ/σe\ell/\sigma_e for the central (Gaussian) mask at several σM\sigma_M, and the pp-vs-p2p^2 spread is largest (reaching ≈0.2\approx 0.2) when the mask width is comparable to the fixation scale (σM∼σe\sigma_M\sim\sigma_e), shrinking for masks much narrower or much wider than it. Which distribution is the right one — and how to estimate the decomposition consistently over it — is the subject of §4; here we only note that the choice is not free once the rate has fixation-scale structure.

2.4 McFarland’s estimator recovers analytical 1−αp1-\alpha^p under (A1)+(A2)

With both assumptions satisfied — constant ntn_t and the homogeneous flat mask — McFarland’s close-pair estimator should return the analytical 1−αp1-\alpha^p of Eq. (11). The flat mask is a clean test target precisely because 1−αp1-\alpha^p traces the full (0,1)(0,1) range as ℓ/σe\ell/\sigma_e is swept, so agreement across the whole sweep is a non-trivial check rather than a single point.

The estimator is unbiased here despite drawing its close pairs from the tighter p2p^2 density, and the reason is the (A2) degeneracy: with a constant mask and a stationary field, 𝔼t[r2(t,e)]\mathbb E_t[r^2(t,e)] does not depend on ee, so the close-pair second moment converges to the same value τ2+μ02\tau^2 + \mu_0^2 no matter where its midpoints land. The rate variance then recovers Vartotalp=τ2\mathrm{Var}_\text{total}^p = \tau^2 and the PSTH variance recovers its pp value τ2ℓ2/(ℓ2+2σe2)\tau^2\ell^2/(\ell^2+2\sigma_e^2), so 1−α→2σe2/(ℓ2+2σe2)=1−αp1-\alpha\to 2\sigma_e^2/(\ell^2+2\sigma_e^2) = 1-\alpha^p — even though the close-pair density is intrinsically p2p^2. This is exactly the degeneracy that breaks once the mask is non-constant and the second moment becomes ee-dependent; that is the subject of §4, and the pp-vs-p2p^2 spread already visible in Fig. 0a is what the naive estimator will get wrong there.

Figure 0b confirms the recovery empirically: across an ℓ/σe\ell/\sigma_e sweep the estimator sits on the analytical 1−αp1-\alpha^p curve (panel A), and the estimate is robust to the close-pair threshold over a useful range (panel B).

The estimator’s consistency (how the seed-to-seed SEM depends on the trials-per-time-bin nn and the number of time bins TT) is more subtle than 1/n1/\sqrt{n}: an across-time-bin noise floor at 2α2/(T−1)\sqrt{2\alpha^2/(T{-}1)} kicks in once within-bin sampling is adequate, and at high SEM the [0,1][0,1] clipping of α\alpha introduces a mean bias. This is treated separately in Appendix §A.9.

3. Extension 1: consistent time-bin weighting (the wtw_t column)

McFarland’s literal estimators implicitly average over analysis time bins with three different weightings (the wtw_t column of the §1.5 table). Under variable ntn_t these diverge and the decomposition is no longer a covariance under any single weighting. The visible consequence is not the per-cell 1−α1-\alpha — where the bias is small — but the cross-cell covariance: the corrected noise correlation is biased downward and the shuffle-null control is driven negative. The culprit is additive, not multiplicative, across-bin structure, and the fix is to pin every term to one weighting.

3.1 McFarland’s literal estimators mix three weightings

Reading the wtw_t column of the §1.5 table off the literal forms:

  • CrateC_\text{rate} (8), pooled over close pairs, weights bin tt by its close-pair count ∝nt(nt−1)/2\propto n_t(n_t{-}1)/2 — pair-count.
  • CpsthC_\text{psth} (6), McFarland’s nested bracket ⟨⟨⋅⟩i≠j⟩t\langle\langle\,\cdot\,\rangle_{i\ne j}\rangle_t, averages within tt then uniformly across bins — wt=1/Tw_t = 1/T.
  • Y‾Y‾⊤\bar Y\bar Y^\top (and CtotalC_\text{total}), the global mean over (i,t)(i,t), weights bin tt by ntn_t — trial-count.

These coincide only when ntn_t is constant. Under the fixRSVP staircase (ntn_t ranging ∼15→360\sim 15\to 360 across bins; Fig. 1A) they diverge — CrateC_\text{rate} emphasizes early high-ntn_t bins, CpsthC_\text{psth} weights every bin equally, Y‾\bar Y sits between — and the LOTC (1) fails term-by-term.

3.2 The consequence is clearest in the shuffle null

The cleanest diagnostic for the weighting mismatch is a shuffle null. Permuting eye trajectories across trials destroys the eye–spike coupling, so the close-pair CrateshufC_\text{rate}^{\text{shuf}} has no real eye dependence and must converge to CpsthC_\text{psth} — provided both use the same wtw_t. The statistic Dz=fz(Cnoisecorr)−fz(Cnoiseuncorr)D_z = f_z(C_\text{noise}^{\text{corr}}) - f_z(C_\text{noise}^{\text{uncorr}}), with Cnoisecorr=Ctotal−CrateshufC_\text{noise}^{\text{corr}} = C_\text{total} - C_\text{rate}^{\text{shuf}} and Cnoiseuncorr=Ctotal−CpsthC_\text{noise}^{\text{uncorr}} = C_\text{total} - C_\text{psth}, then has a known truth of exactly zero — independent of any (A2) effect, since shuffling removes the eye dependence Extension 2 acts on (§4.1). Any deviation is pure weighting mismatch.

On real fixRSVP (Fig. 1D; 25 sessions, 100 shuffles each, cache-only via compute_weighting_data.py) the mixed weighting gives a per-session mean Dzshuf=−0.020D_z^{\text{shuf}} = -0.020, while consistent pair-count weighting pins it to −0.001-0.001. The mismatch falls where it does the most damage — the cross-cell covariance: under mixing the off-diagonal CrateshufC_\text{rate}^{\text{shuf}} exceeds CpsthC_\text{psth}, so the corrected noise covariance is pushed down and the corrected noise correlations are biased negative (median +0.008+0.008 mixed vs +0.019+0.019 consistent). The pre-fix production pipeline lived in exactly this mixed state — close-pair second moment at pair-count, CpsthC_\text{psth} at uniform 1/T1/T, Y‾\bar Y at trial-count — and the fix pins all three to pair-count (note_pipeline.md §6.1).

3.3 Modeling: the bias is additive, not multiplicative

What across-bin structure produces the mismatch? Write the close-pair rate covariance as Crate=MM−Y‾Y‾⊤C_\text{rate} = \mathrm{MM} - \bar Y\bar Y^\top. With the second moment MM\mathrm{MM} pair-count weighted but Y‾\bar Y trial-count weighted, the off-diagonal becomes MMmn−Y‾mtrialY‾ntrial\mathrm{MM}_{mn} - \bar Y_m^{\text{trial}}\bar Y_n^{\text{trial}} instead of the consistent MMmn−Y‾mpairY‾npair\mathrm{MM}_{mn} - \bar Y_m^{\text{pair}}\bar Y_n^{\text{pair}}, an error of Y‾mpairY‾npair−Y‾mtrialY‾ntrial\bar Y^\text{pair}_m\bar Y^\text{pair}_n - \bar Y^\text{trial}_m\bar Y^\text{trial}_n — nonzero exactly when the pair- and trial-count means differ.

A multiplicative gain does not make them differ. In the unified model the field is zero-mean, so a gain envelope α(t)\alpha(t) leaves the mean rate flat at μ0\mu_0 for every tt: Y‾pair=Y‾trial=μ0\bar Y^\text{pair} = \bar Y^\text{trial} = \mu_0, no inflation — a gain change is weighting-invariant. An additive onset transient does. If each cell carries a decaying component gc(t)g_c(t) on top of the field, the pair-count mean — which emphasizes early high-ntn_t bins (Fig. 1A) — exceeds the trial-count mean: δc≡Y‾cpair−Y‾ctrial>0\delta_c \equiv \bar Y^\text{pair}_c - \bar Y^\text{trial}_c > 0 for every cell. The off-diagonal inflation is then ≈μ0(δm+δn)+δmδn\approx \mu_0(\delta_m + \delta_n) + \delta_m\delta_n, positive for every pair — coherent across the population even when the transient amplitudes are random and independent across cells, because the large baseline μ0\mu_0 multiplies each cell’s shift. No cross-cell co-tuning is required; the bias is a property of each cell’s own across-bin mean.

Figure 1C confirms this on a synthetic with a known truth of zero (independent flat cells, so the true noise correlation and the cross-cell PSTH covariance are both zero). With only the multiplicative gain envelope, the mixed and consistent shuffle-null estimators both sit at zero. Adding a random per-cell additive onset transient reproduces the real signature: the mixed estimator goes negative, the consistent one stays at zero. The transient is one plausible, biologically realistic candidate for the real-data bias (V1 onset transients are ubiquitous), not a fixture of the canonical generator; it leaves each cell’s marginal 1−α1-\alpha untouched and acts purely through the across-bin mean.

3.4 Two consistent directions, equivalent in the model; default to ntn_t

Restoring consistency means pinning every term — both second moments and the Y‾\bar Y subtractor — to a single wtw_t. Two principled choices exist: uniform (wt=1/Tw_t = 1/T, McFarland’s literal nested-bracket reading) and pair-count (wt∝nt(nt−1)/2w_t \propto n_t(n_t{-}1)/2, the close-pair estimator’s intrinsic weighting). For the multiplicative structure of the model the two are equivalent: the envelope factor 𝔼w[α2]\mathbb E_w[\alpha^2] cancels in the 1−α1-\alpha ratio (10), so both recover the same closed-form truth (verified across the variable-ntn_t tests). They differ only in efficiency — the close-pair second moment has sampling variance ∝1/|Pt|\propto 1/|P_t| with |Pt|≈nt(nt−1)/2⋅Pr[Δe<ε]|P_t|\approx n_t(n_t{-}1)/2\cdot\Pr[\Delta e<\varepsilon], so the inverse-variance-optimal across-bin combination is pair-count, while uniform gives noisy low-ntn_t bins equal voice. We default to pair-count (ntn_t) for all reported quantities. (An additive transient, §3.3, would make even the truth weakly ww-dependent — a further reason a single weighting must be fixed and reported.)

4. Extension 2: pinning the qq column

Section 3 filled the wtw_t column of the §1.5 table by pinning all three estimators to a single across-bin weighting. This section fills the qq column by pinning all three to a single across-eye distribution. The two extensions are independent — §3 addressed (A1) failure (variable ntn_t); §4 addresses (A2) failure (non-homogeneous stimulus) — and the same natural-vs-stable tradeoff appears on this axis: McFarland’s literal close-pair second moment is pinned to the close-pair conditional density p2p^2, the actual viewing distribution is pp, and §4.2 develops both consistent directions, with their bias/variance tradeoff in §4.3.

4.1 Failures of the naive estimator

Take two trials with eye positions ei,eje_i, e_j drawn independently from pp. The density of close pairs at position ee — pairs with ei≈ej≈ee_i\approx e_j \approx e within the threshold ball — is proportional to p(e)⋅p(e)=p(e)2p(e)\cdot p(e) = p(e)^2. Hence the close-pair estimator (7) does not measure 𝔼e∼p[r2]\mathbb{E}_{e\sim p}[r^2] but

⟨YciYcj∣Δe<ε⟩→𝔼e∼p2[rc(t,e)2](ε→0),p2(e)≡p(e)2∫p(e′)2de′. \big\langle Y_c^i Y_c^j \mid \Delta e<\varepsilon\big\rangle \;\;\longrightarrow\;\; \mathbb{E}_{e\sim p^2}\!\big[r_c(t,e)^2\big] \quad (\varepsilon\to0), \qquad p^2(e) \equiv \frac{p(e)^2}{\int p(e')^2\,de'} .

(12)

For an isotropic Gaussian fixation p=𝒩(0,σe2I)p=\mathcal N(0,\sigma_e^2 I) the close-pair distribution is exactly p2=𝒩(0,σe22I)p^2=\mathcal N\!\big(0,\tfrac{\sigma_e^2}{2}I\big): a tighter, more central Gaussian with half the variance.

Combining §1 with the close-pair density above, in the non-homogeneous case the naive pipeline measures

Ctotal,Cpsthover p(e),Crateover p(e)2, C_{\text{total}},\,C_{\text{psth}} \ \text{over } p(e), \qquad C_{\text{rate}} \ \text{over } p(e)^2 ,

so the derived quantities mix two distributions,

1−α=1−Cpsth(p)Crate(p2),Cnoisecorr=Ctotal(p)−Crate(p2). 1-\alpha = 1 - \frac{C_{\text{psth}}(p)}{C_{\text{rate}}(p^2)}, \qquad C_{\text{noise}}^{\text{corr}} = C_{\text{total}}(p) - C_{\text{rate}}(p^2).

(13)

The LOTC (1) holds term-by-term only when every term is taken over the same distribution. Equation (13) violates this whenever 𝔼p2[r2]≠𝔼p[r2]\mathbb{E}_{p^2}[r^2]\neq\mathbb{E}_p[r^2], i.e. whenever the rate depends on absolute eye position — precisely the non-homogeneous case.

There is a second, subtler defect already present in (7) as written. The close-pair second moment is over p2p^2, but the term subtracted from it — Y‾c2\bar Y_c^2, the global mean — is over pp:

(Crate)ccnaive=𝔼p2[rc2]⏟2nd moment over p2−(𝔼p[rc])2⏟mean over p. \big(C_{\text{rate}}\big)_{cc}^{\text{naive}} = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{p^2}[r_c^2]}_{\text{2nd moment over }p^2} - \underbrace{\big(\mathbb{E}_{p}[r_c]\big)^2}_{\text{mean over }p}.

(14)

This is not a variance under any single distribution. Because 𝔼p2[r]>𝔼p[r]\mathbb{E}_{p^2}[r]>\mathbb{E}_p[r] for a centrally-peaked profile and 𝔼p2[r]<𝔼p[r]\mathbb{E}_{p^2}[r]<\mathbb{E}_p[r] for an eccentric one, the naive CrateC_{\text{rate}} can be inflated above the true p2p^2 variance (central cells) or driven negative (eccentric cells). Figure 2C shows both directions on the closed-form decomposition; §4.2 confirms the empirical recovery.

Note that this is a different axis from Extension 1: Extension 1’s shuffle null is blind to (A2) because shuffling eye trajectories across trials destroys the eye–spike coupling, removing exactly the r(e)r(e) dependence on which the distribution mismatch acts. A shuffle-null Dz≈0D_z\approx 0 therefore says nothing about eye-position consistency.

Figure 2 collects the geometry and its consequence: the central concentration of the close-pair density (A, B), and the resulting closed-form 1−α1-\alpha bias across mask kinds (C). The bias is negligible for the homogeneous flat mask, where 𝔼p2[M2]=𝔼p[M2]\mathbb E_{p^2}[M^2]=\mathbb E_p[M^2] and the decomposition is consistent on either distribution, and grows with the mask’s dependence on absolute eye position.

4.2 The corrected estimator: target distribution as a parameter

The close-pair conditioning is required to cancel the independent Poisson noise, so we cannot avoid sampling from p2p^2 when forming the second-moment estimate. But the close pairs give Poisson-free local estimates of r(t,e)2r(t,e)^2; the bias lives entirely in how those local estimates are aggregated over ee. We re-weight toward a chosen target distribution q(e)q(e) by importance sampling. A sample drawn from pp contributes weight q(e)/p(e)q(e)/p(e); a close pair drawn from p2p^2 contributes weight q(e)/p(e)2q(e)/p(e)^2:

𝔼q[g]̂=∑pairsq(e)p(e)2g∑pairsq(e)p(e)2(second moment),𝔼q[h]̂=∑samplesq(e)p(e)h∑samplesq(e)p(e)(mean, total, PSTH). \widehat{\mathbb{E}_q[g]} = \frac{\sum_{\text{pairs}} \tfrac{q(e)}{p(e)^2}\, g}{\sum_{\text{pairs}} \tfrac{q(e)}{p(e)^2}} \quad\text{(second moment)}, \qquad \widehat{\mathbb{E}_q[h]} = \frac{\sum_{\text{samples}} \tfrac{q(e)}{p(e)}\, h}{\sum_{\text{samples}} \tfrac{q(e)}{p(e)}} \quad\text{(mean, total, PSTH)} .

(15)

Two choices of qq make all terms consistent (module estimators.py, target=):

target qq close-pair weight q/p2q/p^2 sample weight q/pq/p character
Direction 1 (full) pp 1/p1/p (unbounded) 11 actual viewing distribution
Direction 2 (central) p2p^2 11 ∝p\propto p (bounded) central; close-pair-supported
(naive) — 11 on p2p^2 11 (mean over pp) inconsistent (Eq. 14)

Both directions fix the mean/second-moment inconsistency of (14) by construction. They are the two consistent resolutions of the mismatch: push everything out to the full distribution, or pull everything in to the close-pair distribution.

What gets evaluated in practice. The table above already exhibits the key simplification: although the close-pair sampling density is p(e)2p(e)^2, the importance ratios for both consistent targets reduce to expressions in pp alone, so the implementation never has to evaluate p2p^2 (nor its unknown normalizer ∫p2\int p^2) directly. We fit p̂(e)\hat p(e) on the realized per-(trial, time-bin) eye-position pool by Gaussian KDE (scipy.stats.gaussian_kde, Scott’s-rule bandwidth) — for synthetic and real fixRSVP data alike — and evaluate it at close-pair midpoints for Direction 1’s 1/p̂1/\hat p weights or at per-trial positions for Direction 2’s ∝p̂\propto\hat p weights. Both estimators are self-normalized (pw / pw.sum() in the close-pair second moment; w / w.sum() in the weighted mean and covariance helpers of estimators.py), so the unknown p2p^2 normalizer and any uniform mis-scaling of p̂\hat p cancel in numerator and denominator. Shape errors in p̂\hat p do not cancel — they amplify in Direction 1 through the 1/p̂1/\hat p factor wherever close pairs are rare (the periphery), and are the mechanical origin of the variance penalty discussed in §4.3.

Figure 3 confirms the construction on synthetic ground truth. Across an ℓ/σe\ell/\sigma_e sweep for a central mask (left) and an eccentric mask (right), Direction 1 (full) tracks the analytical 1−αp1-\alpha^p and Direction 2 (central) tracks 1−αp21-\alpha^{p^2}, while the naive estimator departs from both in the mask-dependent direction of §4.1 — upward for central, downward for eccentric.

4.3 Direction 1 vs Direction 2: tradeoff and fixation-scale spatial-structure measure

The two consistent directions are not equally easy to estimate. Direction 1 (target pp) requires the unbounded weight 1/p1/p, largest in the periphery — exactly where close pairs are rarest. For an eccentric-modulated cell whose variance lives in the periphery, this makes the estimate noisy; its across-seed standard deviation grows as the threshold shrinks. Direction 2 (target p2p^2) uses bounded weights ∝p\propto p, largest at the center where close pairs are abundant, and is markedly more stable.

  • Direction 1 (pp) is the scientifically natural target. The Fano factor, noise correlation, and FEM fraction are properties of the neuron under the actual viewing conditions, whose eye-position distribution is pp. A quantity reported over p2p^2 is “as if the animal fixated more tightly than it did,” which would require a caveat on every number. We therefore headline Direction 1 for any reported values.
  • Direction 2 (p2p^2) is the stable cross-check. Where Direction 1 is too noisy (eccentric cells, small thresholds, little data), Direction 2’s bounded weights give a reliable, if differently-targeted, estimate.

The gap |(1−α)full−(1−α)central|\lvert(1-\alpha)_{\text{full}} - (1-\alpha)_{\text{central}}\rvert is itself informative — but what it measures is not whether (A2) holds. The unified random-field model under (A2) (flat mask) gives a closed-form gap

(1−αp)−(1−αp2)=σe2ℓ2(ℓ2+2σe2)(ℓ2+σe2), (1{-}\alpha^p) - (1{-}\alpha^{p^2}) = \frac{\sigma_e^2\,\ell^2}{(\ell^2 + 2\sigma_e^2)(\ell^2 + \sigma_e^2)},

(16)

which is non-zero for finite ℓ\ell, vanishing only as ℓ→0\ell\to 0 (decorrelated rates, all FEM under every DD) or ℓ→∞\ell\to\infty (uniform field, no FEM). The maximum is ≈0.17\approx 0.17 at ℓ/σe≈1.18\ell/\sigma_e\approx 1.18 (Fig. 0a A). The gap therefore measures whether the cell’s rate has spatial structure on the fixation scale: small gap when the rate is essentially constant within a fixation (ℓ≫σe\ell\gg\sigma_e) or essentially decorrelated within a fixation (ℓ≪σe\ell\ll\sigma_e); large gap when the rate’s spatial scale and the fixation scale are comparable. Non-homogeneous masks (central, eccentric) add to this baseline.

The gap is still a useful empirical signal — it tells you that the choice of eye-position distribution DD matters for the reported 1−α1-\alpha — but it is not a clean test of (A2).

4.4 Multi-bin eye trajectories: the production-setting extension

§4.2 framed the close-pair filter as a single-bin condition |ei−ej|<ε\lvert e_i-e_j\rvert<\varepsilon — and the §4.5 real-data analysis honours that framing by using the eye position at one analysis time bin per sample. The production covariance pipeline (VisionCore/covariance.py::compute_eye_distances) instead works on TT-bin trajectories: each sample ii carries an eye trajectory τi=(ei,1,…,ei,T)\tau_i = (e_{i,1},\ldots,e_{i,T}) over a window of T=thist+tcountT = t_{\mathrm{hist}}+t_{\mathrm{count}} bins, and the close-pair filter is the root-mean-square trajectory distance

∥τi−τj∥RMS=1T∑t=1T∥ei,t−ej,t∥2<ε \big\lVert\tau_i-\tau_j\big\rVert_{\mathrm{RMS}} \;=\; \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\lVert e_{i,t}-e_{j,t}\rVert^2} \;<\;\varepsilon

(17)

so that two trials are “close” only when their whole trajectories — not just the spike-count bin — are similar. The motivation is that the neuron integrates over a temporal window, so the response context that a close pair has to share is the whole window, not the instantaneous eye position.

This breaks the §4.2 importance-weight construction as written. The close-pair density now lives on ℝ2T\mathbb R^{2T} — the trajectory density p(τ)p(\tau) squared, restricted to ∥τi−τj∥RMS<ε\lVert\tau_i-\tau_j\rVert_{\mathrm{RMS}}<\varepsilon — and fitting a density in ℝ2T\mathbb R^{2T} for typical T∈[10,30]T\in[10,30] is the curse of dimensionality. We cannot simply lift the §4.5 single-bin KDE to the trajectory and call it done.

Our extension. Build the importance weight from two 2-D KDEs:

p̂marg(e)=KDE({ei,t}i=1…N,t=1…T),p̂cp,marg(e)=KDE({mk,t}k=1…P,t=1…T) \hat p_{\mathrm{marg}}(e)\,=\,\mathrm{KDE}\big(\{e_{i,t}\}_{i=1\dots N,\,t=1\dots T}\big), \qquad \hat p_{cp,\mathrm{marg}}(e)\,=\,\mathrm{KDE}\big(\{m_{k,t}\}_{k=1\dots P,\,t=1\dots T}\big)

(18)

where p̂marg\hat p_{\mathrm{marg}} pools per-bin positions across all samples and p̂cp,marg\hat p_{cp,\mathrm{marg}} pools per-bin positions of the close-pair midpoint trajectories mk=12(τi+τj)m_k=\tfrac12(\tau_i+\tau_j) (one midpoint trajectory per close pair kk, contributing TT per-bin positions to the pool). The §4.2 importance weights are then evaluated at each trajectory’s centroid ci=1T∑tei,tc_i=\tfrac1T\sum_t e_{i,t}:

target per-sample weight at cic_i per-pair weight at cmid=12(ci+cj)c_{\mathrm{mid}}=\tfrac12(c_i+c_j)
Direction 1 (full) 11 p̂marg(cmid)/p̂cp,marg(cmid)\hat p_{\mathrm{marg}}(c_{\mathrm{mid}})\,/\,\hat p_{cp,\mathrm{marg}}(c_{\mathrm{mid}})
Direction 2 (central) p̂cp,marg(ci)/p̂marg(ci)\hat p_{cp,\mathrm{marg}}(c_i)\,/\,\hat p_{\mathrm{marg}}(c_i) 11
naive 11 11

(estimators.decompose_trajectory).

Why this works in the flat-trajectory limit. Decompose ei,t=ci+ξi,te_{i,t}=c_i+\xi_{i,t} with ξi,t∼𝒩(0,σdrift2I)\xi_{i,t}\sim\mathcal N(0,\sigma_{\mathrm{drift}}^2 I) i.i.d. across tt and centroid ci∼pcentroidc_i\sim p_{\mathrm{centroid}}. As σdrift→0\sigma_{\mathrm{drift}}\to 0 the trajectory collapses to its centroid, so

p̂marg(e)→pcentroid(e),p̂cp,marg(e)∝pcentroid(e)2 \hat p_{\mathrm{marg}}(e) \;\to\; p_{\mathrm{centroid}}(e), \qquad \hat p_{cp,\mathrm{marg}}(e) \;\propto\; p_{\mathrm{centroid}}(e)^2

(19)

(the latter because in the flat limit pcp(τ)∝p(τ)2p_{cp}(\tau)\propto p(\tau)^2 is supported on constant trajectories with weight pcentroid(c)2p_{\mathrm{centroid}}(c)^2, and pooling per-bin positions across midpoint trajectories collapses to a single pcentroid2p_{\mathrm{centroid}}^2 KDE). The ratio p̂cp,marg/p̂marg\hat p_{cp,\mathrm{marg}}/\hat p_{\mathrm{marg}} at the centroid then collapses to pcentroid(c)p_{\mathrm{centroid}}(c) up to a normalising constant, and the table above recovers §4.2’s Direction 1 pair weight 1/pcentroid(c)1/p_{\mathrm{centroid}}(c) and Direction 2 sample weight ∝pcentroid(c)\propto p_{\mathrm{centroid}}(c) exactly. Self-normalisation absorbs the normalising constant in numerator and denominator.

The flat-trajectory approximation. For σdrift>0\sigma_{\mathrm{drift}}>0 the estimator targets the per-bin marginal ppb(e)=pcentroid*ϕσdrift(e)p_{\mathrm{pb}}(e)=p_{\mathrm{centroid}}*\phi_{\sigma_{\mathrm{drift}}}(e) — a Gaussian-smoothed version of the centroid distribution. The “actual viewing distribution” the estimator aims at is therefore 𝒩(0,σtraj2I)\mathcal N(0,\sigma_{\mathrm{traj}}^2 I) with σtraj2=σe2+σdrift2\sigma_{\mathrm{traj}}^2=\sigma_e^2+\sigma_{\mathrm{drift}}^2, and the truth to compare against is §4.2’s closed form at σtraj\sigma_{\mathrm{traj}} (synthetic.ground_truth(kind, sqrt(sigma_e^2+sigma_drift^2), ell, sigma_M=sigma_M)). The construction is exact in expectation in the flat limit; for non-zero drift the residual comes from two sources:

  1. Centroid-vs-per-bin smoothing. Evaluating the ratio at the trajectory centroid (a single 2-D point) discards within-window drift; the ratio at cic_i is biased by an amount that scales with σdrift/σe\sigma_{\mathrm{drift}}/\sigma_e.
  2. Threshold inflation. Because E[∥τi−τj∥RMS2]≈dcentroid2+4σdrift2E[\lVert\tau_i-\tau_j\rVert^2_{\mathrm{RMS}}] \approx d_{\mathrm{centroid}}^2 + 4\sigma_{\mathrm{drift}}^2, the RMS-trajectory threshold ε\varepsilon must grow with σdrift\sigma_{\mathrm{drift}} to admit any close pairs at all; at large σdrift\sigma_{\mathrm{drift}} the close-pair filter loses selectivity and the central-region concentration of the close-pair midpoint density weakens.

Validation (Fig. 4). A controlled synthetic with explicit σdrift\sigma_{\mathrm{drift}} knob (synthetic.make_trajectory_session) shows the flat-limit recovery is sharp and the bias grows smoothly with σdrift/σe\sigma_{\mathrm{drift}}/\sigma_e. At σdrift/σe=0\sigma_{\mathrm{drift}}/\sigma_e=0 the corrected Directions 1 and 2 sit on their respective truths within seed noise (panel E); at σdrift/σe∼0.2\sigma_{\mathrm{drift}}/\sigma_e\sim 0.2 — comparable to the operating regime for fixational drift over a typical thist+tcountt_{\mathrm{hist}}+t_{\mathrm{count}} window — the bias is small; at σdrift/σe∼1\sigma_{\mathrm{drift}}/\sigma_e\sim 1 the trajectories are no longer “essentially flat” and the bias is visible but bounded. Panels B–D show the §4.1 mechanism reappearing in the multi-bin setting: p̂cp,marg\hat p_{cp,\mathrm{marg}} is narrower than p̂marg\hat p_{\mathrm{marg}}, and their ratio peaks at the centre — the close pairs over-represent the high-density region exactly as the single-bin §4.1 picture predicts.

The trajectory-mode estimator is the production-setting bridge for §4.5’s single-bin analysis: when the §6.2 production change lands (note_pipeline.md), the same target ∈ {'naive','full','central'} parameter selects the same three behaviours; the §4.5 numbers are recovered as the σdrift→0\sigma_{\mathrm{drift}}\to 0, T=1T=1 limit; and the curse-of-dimensionality wall that the multi-bin filter raised is sidestepped by replacing the trajectory density with two 2-D KDEs evaluated at the trajectory centroid.

A note on the centred close-pair second moment

The implementation in decompose_trajectory computes the close-pair second moment on centred counts S−r̂S - \hat r instead of the usual uncentred SiSj⊤S_i S_j^\top followed by an r̂r̂⊤\hat r\hat r^\top subtraction. The motivation is numerical: when r̂⋅twindow\hat r \cdot t_\text{window} dominates the magnitude of SS, the literal “second moment minus r̂r̂⊤\hat r\hat r^\top” can be a catastrophic cancellation, whereas centring first turns each pair product into a small-times-small operation. This matters on the §4.4 synthetic validation, where r̂\hat r is a known constant and the precision argument is clean.

The two forms are related by the identity

∑(i,j) closewij(Si−r̂)(Sj−r̂)⊤=MM̂−r̂Y‾w⊤−X‾wr̂⊤+r̂r̂⊤,\sum_{(i,j) \text{ close}} w_{ij}(S_i - \hat r)(S_j - \hat r)^\top = \widehat{MM} - \hat r\,\bar Y_w^\top - \bar X_w\,\hat r^\top + \hat r\hat r^\top,

where MM̂=∑wijSiSj⊤/∑wij\widehat{MM} = \sum w_{ij} S_i S_j^\top / \sum w_{ij} is the weighted close-pair second moment and X‾w=∑wijSi/∑wij\bar X_w = \sum w_{ij} S_i / \sum w_{ij}, Y‾w=∑wijSj/∑wij\bar Y_w = \sum w_{ij} S_j / \sum w_{ij} are the weighted close-pair-set sample means of the left and right cell. They collapse to the uncentred form MM̂−r̂r̂⊤\widehat{MM} - \hat r\hat r^\top if and only if X‾w=Y‾w=r̂\bar X_w = \bar Y_w = \hat r — that is, if the mean we subtract equals the close-pair-set sample mean.

For the consistent targets (full, central) the importance reweighting makes X‾w\bar X_w and r̂\hat r both estimate the same population quantity (𝔼p[r]\mathbb{E}_p[r] for full, 𝔼p2[r]\mathbb{E}_{p^2}[r] for central) — but they remain distinct estimators in finite samples: r̂\hat r averages all samples under the per-sample weighting (low variance), while X‾w\bar X_w averages the close-pair subset under the per-pair weighting (noisier — the close-pair pool is sparse and the per-pair weights for full are a density-ratio that the KDE only estimates). The centred and uncentred forms therefore agree asymptotically (both consistent for CrateqC_\text{rate}^q) but differ in finite samples by an amount controlled by ∥r̂−X‾w∥∥r̂∥\|\hat r - \bar X_w\|\,\|\hat r\|. On the note_pipeline.md §7 real data this gap is non-negligible — e.g. at tcount=2t_\text{count}=2 the median 1−αfull1-\alpha_\text{full} shifts from 0.710.71 (centred) to 0.770.77 (uncentred), and similarly for central.

For the inconsistent target naive the gap is also non-zero, and for a different reason: r̂\hat r estimates 𝔼p[r]\mathbb{E}_p[r] while X‾w\bar X_w estimates 𝔼p2[r]\mathbb{E}_{p^2}[r], so the two are not even equal in expectation. The centred and uncentred naive estimators are therefore two different inconsistent estimators of the §4.1 mixed quantity, with neither privileged.

The note_pipeline.md §7 pipeline uses the uncentred form for all three targets for two reasons:

  1. §4.5 reference compatibility. The cell-side single-bin analysis in §4.5 (generate_realdata.py, Fig. 5) ran on the single-bin estimators.decompose, which is uncentred. The note_pipeline.md §7 multi-cell, multi-window pipeline numbers must extend §4.5 rather than redefine it, and this requires the same close-pair Crate semantic.
  2. note_pipeline.md §7.2 equivalence audit. Legacy compute_conditional_second_moments is uncentred, so target='naive' exact equivalence requires the uncentred form.

The centred form remains the default in estimators.decompose_trajectory because the §4.4 synthetic validation relies on its precision claim. Future work that promotes the trajectory- mode estimator to production should decide centred vs uncentred per target on its own finite-sample merits — the two are not interchangeable on real data even where both are consistent.

4.5 Consequences on real data (fixRSVP, cache-only)

We applied the matched estimator to the real fixRSVP recordings, cache-only (no GPU, no model inference; generate_realdata.py reads the Figure-4 cache of trial-aligned spikes and real eye trajectories). 1−α1-\alpha and the Fano factor are computed on the real spikes with each cell’s own validity mask (reproducing the Figure-2 per-cell 1−α1-\alpha at the median); the eye-position density is a Gaussian KDE of the measured fixational positions. This implementation uses a single-bin close-pair filter (the eye position at one analysis time bin per sample), so the §4.2 importance weights apply without modification; the multi-bin trajectory extension required by the production filter (§4.4) is folded into the gated §6.2 pipeline change (note_pipeline.md). Pooled over 397 good cells (ccmax>0.85\mathrm{cc}_{\max}>0.85, 2 monkeys, 24 sessions):

quantity naive Direction 1 (pp, full) Direction 2 (p2p^2, central)
median 1−α1-\alpha 0.734 0.702 0.608
median Fano 0.846 0.875 —
  • The naive bias on population 1−α1-\alpha is small. The naive median (0.734) reproduces the Figure-2 value (0.732) and lies only +0.022+0.022 above the Direction-1-corrected value (0.702). On the actual-viewing target pp, the existing Figure-2 / Figure-4 panel-D 1−α1-\alpha conclusions are therefore robust to the (A2) distribution mismatch at the population level — real V1 cells do not behave like the extreme synthetic central/eccentric profiles, which suffer large biases.
  • The gap measures fixation-scale spatial structure, and it is measurable. The gap between the two consistent targets, |(1−α)full−(1−α)central|\lvert(1-\alpha)_{\text{full}} - (1-\alpha)_{\text{central}}\rvert, has a population median of 0.089 with a tail beyond 0.30.3 (Fig. 5B). Under the unified random-field model the gap is non-zero under (A2) itself when ℓ∼σe\ell\sim\sigma_e (Eq. 16) — peaking at ≈0.17\approx 0.17 — so gap =0.089= 0.089 is evidence that the cells’ rate maps have spatial structure on the fixation scale, which is expected for any cell with a finite spatial RF, not necessarily that (A2) is violated. What the gap does say is that the choice of eye-position distribution DD matters for the reported 1−α1-\alpha at this scale: a ±0.09\pm 0.09 swing between Direction 1 and Direction 2 is the order of the Direction-1-vs-naive bias we found above (−0.022-0.022).
  • The Fano factor shifts modestly under matching (median 0.846→0.8750.846\to0.875, +3%+3\%), consistent with the synthetic prediction that the Fano factor inherits the rate-variance distribution mismatch; per-cell shifts are larger.

The noise-correlation consequence on real spikes is small at the population level — both on the unified synthetic (naive vs matched median |r||r| differ by ≈0.005\approx 0.005) and consistent with the modest Fano shift here (0.846→0.8750.846\to 0.875). Quantifying it directly on real spike pairs requires either the full windowed pipeline or careful joint-pair validity masking; because it shares the same pipeline change, it is folded into the gated Figure-2 fix (note_pipeline.md §6) rather than approximated here.

Appendix: derivations

The closed-form decomposition and its three mask evaluations come first (§A.1–§A.4), followed by the sampling and weighting derivations the main text relies on (§A.5–§A.8), the finite-sample consistency analysis (§A.9), and the PSTH covariance estimator (§A.10).

A.1 Closed-form decomposition for the unified rate field

With the unified rate equation (9), the across-time-bin and across-eye distributions decouple by linearity. Write Xt(e)=M(e)α(t)st(e)X_t(e) = M(e)\,\alpha(t)\,s_t(e), so r=μ0+Xr = \mu_0 + X. The field is zero-mean, 𝔼[st(e)]=0\mathbb E[s_t(e)] = 0, with covariance K(δ)=τ2exp(−∥δ∥2/(2ℓ2))K(\delta) = \tau^2\exp(-\lVert\delta\rVert^2/(2\ell^2)) and independent draws across time bins.

Mean. 𝔼t,e∼D[r]=μ0\mathbb E_{t,e\sim D}[r] = \mu_0 for any w,Dw, D: the field is zero-mean and the mean is constant in ee.

Total variance. VartotalD,w=𝔼[X2]=𝔼w[α2]𝔼D[M2]𝔼s[st(e)2]=𝔼w[α2]τ2𝔼D[M2]\mathrm{Var}_\text{total}^{D,w} = \mathbb E[X^2] = \mathbb E_w[\alpha^2]\,\mathbb E_D[M^2]\,\mathbb E_s[s_t(e)^2] = \mathbb E_w[\alpha^2]\,\tau^2\,\mathbb E_D[M^2].

PSTH variance. Let GtD=𝔼e∼D[M(e)st(e)]G_t^D = \mathbb E_{e\sim D}[M(e)\,s_t(e)], so the per-bin PSTH is 𝔼e∼D[rt(e)]=μ0+α(t)GtD\mathbb E_{e\sim D}[r_t(e)] = \mu_0 + \alpha(t)\,G_t^D. The GtDG_t^D are i.i.d. across time bins with 𝔼[GtD]=0\mathbb E[G_t^D] = 0 and 𝔼[(GtD)2]=IM,K,D\mathbb E[(G_t^D)^2] = I_{M,K,D}, so in the large-TT limit

VarPSTHD,w=𝔼w[α2]IM,K,D,IM,K,D=∬M(e1)M(e2)K(e1−e2)D(e1)D(e2)de1de2. \mathrm{Var}_\text{PSTH}^{D,w} = \mathbb E_w[\alpha^2]\,I_{M,K,D}, \qquad I_{M,K,D} = \iint M(e_1)\,M(e_2)\,K(e_1{-}e_2)\,D(e_1)\,D(e_2)\,de_1\,de_2.

The ratio 1−αD,w=1−IM,K,D/(τ2𝔼D[M2])1-\alpha^{D,w} = 1 - I_{M,K,D}/(\tau^2\,\mathbb E_D[M^2]) is invariant under time-bin weighting and the envelope α(t)\alpha(t): both VarPSTH\mathrm{Var}_\text{PSTH} and Vartotal\mathrm{Var}_\text{total} carry the same factor 𝔼w[α2]\mathbb E_w[\alpha^2], which cancels. Both Extension-1 directions target the same closed-form ratio; the choice between them is a finite-sample efficiency question, not a bias question.

A Gaussian two-point integral. Every mask below is built from Gaussian factors, so IM,K,DI_{M,K,D} reduces to products of a single one-dimensional two-point Gaussian integral. Take D=𝒩(0,s2I)D = \mathcal N(0, s^2 I) in ℝ2\mathbb R^2; the integrand factorises across the two spatial dimensions. The per-dimension building block, carrying an extra Gaussian precision c1,c2c_1, c_2 on its two points, is

T(c1,c2)=∬e−12c1x12e−12c2x22e−(x1−x2)2/(2ℓ2)𝒩(x1;0,s2)𝒩(x2;0,s2)dx1dx2=1s2detΛ, T(c_1, c_2) = \iint e^{-\frac12 c_1 x_1^2}\,e^{-\frac12 c_2 x_2^2}\, e^{-(x_1-x_2)^2/(2\ell^2)}\,\mathcal N(x_1;0,s^2)\,\mathcal N(x_2;0,s^2)\, dx_1\,dx_2 = \frac{1}{s^2\sqrt{\det\Lambda}},

Λ=(p1+ℓ−2−ℓ−2−ℓ−2p2+ℓ−2),pk=1s2+ck,detΛ=p1p2+p1+p2ℓ2. \Lambda = \begin{pmatrix} p_1 + \ell^{-2} & -\ell^{-2} \\ -\ell^{-2} & p_2 + \ell^{-2}\end{pmatrix}, \quad p_k = \frac1{s^2} + c_k, \quad \det\Lambda = p_1 p_2 + \frac{p_1 + p_2}{\ell^2}.

The result follows by collecting the exponent into the quadratic form 12x⊤Λx\tfrac12 x^\top\Lambda x and using ∫e−12x⊤Λxdx1dx2=2π/detΛ\int e^{-\frac12 x^\top\Lambda x}\,dx_1\,dx_2 = 2\pi/\sqrt{\det\Lambda} against the 1/(2πs2)1/(2\pi s^2) from the two 𝒩(⋅;0,s2)\mathcal N(\cdot;0,s^2) normalisers. A Gaussian mask factor exp(−∥e∥2/(2σM2))\exp(-\lVert e\rVert^2/(2\sigma_M^2)) on a point sets c=1/σM2c = 1/\sigma_M^2 there; its absence sets c=0c = 0. When the two-point product M(e1)M(e2)M(e_1)M(e_2) expands into a sum of such Gaussian-factored terms, the full two-point integral is

IM,K,D=τ2∑terms(±)[T(c1,c2)]2, I_{M,K,D} = \tau^2 \sum_{\text{terms}} (\pm)\,\big[T(c_1, c_2)\big]^2,

one squared TT per term (the square is the product over the two spatial dimensions). The viewing and close-pair distributions enter only through s2s^2: s2=σe2s^2 = \sigma_e^2 for D=pD = p and s2=σe2/2s^2 = \sigma_e^2/2 for D=p2D = p^2.

A.2 Flat mask

For M≡1M \equiv 1 both points carry c=0c = 0, so IM,K,D/τ2=T(0,0)2=ℓ2/(ℓ2+2s2)I_{M,K,D}/\tau^2 = T(0,0)^2 = \ell^2/(\ell^2 + 2s^2) and 𝔼D[M2]=1\mathbb E_D[M^2] = 1. With s2=σe2s^2 = \sigma_e^2 and s2=σe2/2s^2 = \sigma_e^2/2 this gives

1−αp=2σe2ℓ2+2σe2,1−αp2=σe2ℓ2+σe2, 1-\alpha^p = \frac{2\sigma_e^2}{\ell^2 + 2\sigma_e^2}, \qquad 1-\alpha^{p^2} = \frac{\sigma_e^2}{\ell^2 + \sigma_e^2},

and the (A2)-respecting gap

(1−αp)−(1−αp2)=σe2ℓ2(ℓ2+2σe2)(ℓ2+σe2), (1{-}\alpha^p) - (1{-}\alpha^{p^2}) = \frac{\sigma_e^2\,\ell^2}{(\ell^2 + 2\sigma_e^2)(\ell^2 + \sigma_e^2)},

whose maximum sits at ℓ/σe=21/4≈1.19\ell/\sigma_e = 2^{1/4} \approx 1.19 (differentiate in ℓ2\ell^2 and set to zero).

A.3 Central mask

For M(e)=exp(−∥e∥2/(2σM2))M(e) = \exp(-\lVert e\rVert^2/(2\sigma_M^2)) both points carry the mask, c1=c2=1/σM2c_1 = c_2 = 1/\sigma_M^2, so IM,K,D/τ2=T(1/σM2,1/σM2)2I_{M,K,D}/\tau^2 = T(1/\sigma_M^2,1/\sigma_M^2)^2 and 𝔼D[M2]=σM2/(σM2+2s2)\mathbb E_D[M^2] = \sigma_M^2/(\sigma_M^2 + 2s^2). Collecting the algebra into the effective convolution scale σeff2=s2σM2/(s2+σM2)\sigma_{\mathrm{eff}}^2 = s^2\sigma_M^2/(s^2 + \sigma_M^2),

IM,K,D=τ2(σM2s2+σM2)2ℓ2ℓ2+2σeff2,1−αD=1−IM,K,Dτ2𝔼D[M2], I_{M,K,D} = \tau^2 \left(\frac{\sigma_M^2}{s^2 + \sigma_M^2}\right)^{2} \frac{\ell^2}{\ell^2 + 2\sigma_{\mathrm{eff}}^2}, \qquad 1-\alpha^{D} = 1 - \frac{I_{M,K,D}}{\tau^2\,\mathbb E_D[M^2]},

with s2=σe2s^2 = \sigma_e^2 for D=pD = p and σe2/2\sigma_e^2/2 for D=p2D = p^2 (synthetic._central_one_minus_alpha_closed_form).

A.4 Eccentric mask

For M(e)=1−g(e)M(e) = 1 - g(e) with g(e)=exp(−∥e∥2/(2σM2))g(e) = \exp(-\lVert e\rVert^2/(2\sigma_M^2)), the two-point product expands as M(e1)M(e2)=1−g1−g2+g1g2M(e_1)M(e_2) = 1 - g_1 - g_2 + g_1 g_2 and both moments split into Gaussian pieces. The second moment is

𝔼D[M2]=1−2𝔼D[g]+𝔼D[g2],𝔼D[g]=σM2σM2+s2,𝔼D[g2]=σM2σM2+2s2. \mathbb E_D[M^2] = 1 - 2\,\mathbb E_D[g] + \mathbb E_D[g^2], \qquad \mathbb E_D[g] = \frac{\sigma_M^2}{\sigma_M^2 + s^2}, \quad \mathbb E_D[g^2] = \frac{\sigma_M^2}{\sigma_M^2 + 2s^2}.

Each term of the expansion carries the mask on neither, one, or both points, mapping onto T(0,0)T(0,0), T(c,0)T(c,0) and T(c,c)T(c,c) with c=1/σM2c = 1/\sigma_M^2:

IM,K,Dτ2=T(0,0)2−2T(c,0)2+T(c,c)2,1−αD=1−IM,K,Dτ2𝔼D[M2] \frac{I_{M,K,D}}{\tau^2} = T(0,0)^2 - 2\,T(c,0)^2 + T(c,c)^2, \qquad 1-\alpha^{D} = 1 - \frac{I_{M,K,D}}{\tau^2\,\mathbb E_D[M^2]}

(the two cross terms −g1-g_1 and −g2-g_2 are equal by symmetry, hence the factor of two). This is synthetic._eccentric_one_minus_alpha_closed_form, and test_eccentric_mask_closed_form_matches_mc checks it against a 2×1062\times10^6-sample Monte-Carlo evaluation of the same ratio to <3×10−3<3\times10^{-3} across mask widths and both distributions.

A.5 Close-pair density is p(e)2p(e)^2

This short derivation has been lifted into the main text (§1.5, the “q=p2q = p^2” bullet): for ei,ej∼iidpe_i,e_j\stackrel{\text{iid}}{\sim}p the probability that a pair is “close” with both members in a small ball Bδ(e)B_\delta(e) is ≈(p(e)|Bδ|)2\approx\big(p(e)\,|B_\delta|\big)^2, so the close-pair position density is p(e)2/∫p2p(e)^2/\int p^2, which for p=𝒩(0,σe2I)p=\mathcal N(0,\sigma_e^2 I) is 𝒩(0,σe22I)\mathcal N(0,\tfrac{\sigma_e^2}{2}I) — variance halved.

A.6 Importance weights

To estimate 𝔼q[g]=∫gq\mathbb E_q[g] = \int g\,q from samples distributed as ss, write 𝔼q[g]=∫gqss=𝔼s[gqs]\mathbb E_q[g] = \int g\,\tfrac{q}{s}\,s = \mathbb E_s\! \big[g\,\tfrac{q}{s}\big], so each sample is weighted by q/sq/s. For the second moment the close pairs have s=p2s=p^2, giving weight q/p2q/p^2 (Direction 1: p/p2=1/pp/p^2=1/p; Direction 2: p2/p2=1p^2/p^2=1). For the mean/total/PSTH the trials have s=ps=p, giving weight q/pq/p (Direction 1: 11; Direction 2: pp). Substituting into the empirical LOTC terms yields a decomposition in which all of CtotalC_{\text{total}}, CpsthC_{\text{psth}} and CrateC_{\text{rate}} are taken over the single distribution qq, restoring term-by-term consistency of (1).

A.7 Two readings of the nested-bracket close-pair estimator

The close-pair second-moment estimator (7) is written ⟨⟨YciYcj∣Δe<ε⟩i≠j⟩t\langle\langle Y_c^iY_c^j \mid \Delta e<\varepsilon\rangle_{i\ne j}\rangle_t. Two readings of the nested bracket give two consistent across-bin weightings.

Inner-then-outer (uniform 1/T1/T). Read the brackets literally as two stages: at each bin tt, average the close-pair products to get a per-bin mean m‾t=|Pt|−1∑(i,j)∈PtYiYj\bar m_t = |P_t|^{-1}\sum_{(i,j)\in P_t} Y^iY^j; then average the {m‾t}\{\bar m_t\} uniformly across the TT bins. Each bin contributes wt=1/Tw_t = 1/T regardless of |Pt||P_t|. This is the natural reading of “averaging across time points” — McFarland’s text on p.6228.

Pool-then-average (pair-count ∝nt(nt−1)/2\propto n_t(n_t{-}1)/2). Pool all close pairs from all bins into a single flat list and average uniformly per pair. Bin tt then contributes |Pt|/∑t′|Pt′||P_t|/\sum_{t'}|P_{t'}|, which under uniform eye sampling is ≈nt(nt−1)/2\approx n_t(n_t{-}1)/2 normalized.

Under constant ntn_t the two readings coincide (|Pt||P_t| is the same for every tt). Under variable ntn_t they differ — the bin with the most close pairs either gets the same voice as every other (uniform) or dominates the average (pair-count). Both are consistent across-bin weightings for the LOTC, and §3 develops the bias/variance tradeoff between them.

A.8 When the corrections are a no-op

  • Extension 1. If ntn_t is constant across time bins, the uniform and pair-count directions coincide (the two readings of §A.7 give the same weight per bin), and Extension 1 reduces to the identity.
  • Extension 2. The correction is the identity whenever (A2) holds at the second moment: 𝔼t[r2(t,e)]\mathbb E_t[r^2(t,e)] independent of ee makes 𝔼e∼D[𝔼t[r2]]\mathbb E_{e\sim D}\!\big[\mathbb E_t[r^2]\big] the same for every DD, so the close-pair second moment converges to the right Vartotal\mathrm{Var}_\text{total} regardless of its p2p^2 sampling. This reproduces McFarland’s stated regime. Note however that even under (A2), VarPSTHD\mathrm{Var}_\text{PSTH}^D does depend on DD through the eye- distribution-spread of the PSTH integrand, so the Direction-1 and Direction-2 targets do not coincide in 1−α1-\alpha; their gap is the fixation-scale spatial-structure measure of §4.3. The test_homogeneous_mask_correction_is_noop_for_full_target test confirms that under (A2) (flat mask), target='naive' and target='full' agree on 1−αp1-\alpha^p while target='central' recovers 1−αp21-\alpha^{p^2}.

A.9 Consistency: how sd[1−α̂]\mathrm{sd}[1-\hat\alpha] depends on NN and TT, and the [0,1][0,1] clipping bias

Section 2.4 noted that the seed-to-seed SEM of McFarland’s estimator does not shrink as 1/N1/\sqrt{N} alone — an across-time-bin noise floor in TT kicks in once within-bin sampling is adequate, and at high SEM the [0,1][0,1] clipping of α̂\hat\alpha introduces a mean bias. This appendix derives both effects on the unified flat-mask synthetic under (A1)+(A2) and calibrates them empirically against the closed form. The code is fig_consistency.py; the empirical sweep is cached to consistency_sweep.npz.

A.9.1 The across-time-bin floor

Take the flat mask (M≡1M\equiv 1), constant nt=Nn_t = N, and deterministic rates (no observation noise). The per-time-bin PSTH is 𝔼e∼p[r(t,e)]=μ0+Gt\mathbb E_{e\sim p}[r(t,e)] = \mu_0 + G_t with

Gt=∫st(e)p(e)de. G_t = \int s_t(e)\,p(e)\,de.

By (A2) the {Gt}t=1T\{G_t\}_{t=1}^{T} are i.i.d. zero-mean across time bins with

Vp≡𝔼Gt2=∬K(e1−e2)p(e1)p(e2)de1de2=τ2ℓ2ℓ2+2σe2=α*τ2,α*≡ℓ2ℓ2+2σe2. V_p \;\equiv\; \mathbb E\,G_t^2 \;=\; \iint K(e_1-e_2)\,p(e_1)\,p(e_2)\,de_1\,de_2 \;=\; \frac{\tau^2\,\ell^2}{\ell^2+2\sigma_e^2} \;=\; \alpha^*\,\tau^2, \qquad \alpha^* \equiv \frac{\ell^2}{\ell^2+2\sigma_e^2}.

In the large-NN limit the close-pair pool (∼T⋅N(N−1)/2\sim T\cdot N(N{-}1)/2 pairs) makes Ĉrate→τ2\widehat C_{\text{rate}}\to \tau^2 deterministically, so the estimator collapses to

α̂≈1τ2⋅1T−1∑t(Gt−G‾)2, \hat\alpha \;\approx\; \frac{1}{\tau^2}\cdot\frac{1}{T-1}\sum_t\!(G_t-\bar G)^2,

the sample variance of TT i.i.d. Gaussians divided by their (assumed known) population variance scaling. Its sampling variance is

Var[α̂]=1τ4⋅2Vp2T−1=2α*2T−1, \mathrm{Var}[\hat\alpha] \;=\; \frac{1}{\tau^4}\cdot\frac{2\,V_p^2}{T-1} \;=\; \frac{2\,{\alpha^*}^2}{T-1},

giving the across-time-bin SEM floor

limN→∞sd[1−α̂]=α*2T−1, \lim_{N\to\infty} \mathrm{sd}[1-\hat\alpha] \;=\; \alpha^*\,\sqrt{\frac{2}{T-1}},

(A6.1)

a finite limit in TT alone. At ℓ=σe\ell=\sigma_e (so α*=1/3\alpha^*=1/3) and T=100T=100, (A6.1) gives sd≈0.0474\mathrm{sd}\approx 0.0474, in agreement with the leveling-off observed in §2.4.

A.9.2 Within-bin contribution and the empirical decomposition

At finite NN, the close-pair estimator’s local fluctuations and the finite-trial sampling of GtG_t each contribute a within-bin noise term that scales as 1/N1/\sqrt N at fixed TT. Treating the across-time-bin and within-bin contributions as approximately independent,

sd[1−α̂]≈sdwithin2(N)+sdfloor2(T), \mathrm{sd}[1-\hat\alpha] \;\approx\; \sqrt{\;\mathrm{sd}_{\text{within}}^2(N) \;+\; \mathrm{sd}_{\text{floor}}^2(T)\;},

(A6.2)

so adding trials drives the SD down toward the floor but does not pierce it. Fig. A6A shows the heatmap of empirical sd[1−α̂]\mathrm{sd}[1-\hat\alpha] over (N,T)(N,T) at ℓ=σe\ell=\sigma_e: the right edge (large TT, sd≈0.04\mathrm{sd}\approx 0.04) is dominated by the floor; the left edge (small TT) is dominated by the floor as well — across the whole grid the floor sets the bottom of the achievable SD. Fig. A6C shows the same data sliced at fixed NN: the N=800N=800 points sit on the analytical floor curve α*2/(T−1)\alpha^*\sqrt{2/(T-1)} at large TT and rise above it at small TT where within-bin noise becomes significant relative to the floor.

The practical implication is that for a target SEM on 1−α̂1-\hat\alpha, more time bins is the only binding knob once trials/bin is large enough to drop within-bin noise below the floor. In fixRSVP with T∼100T\sim 100 post-fix bins, α*=1/3\alpha^*=1/3 gives a floor of ≈0.047\approx 0.047, comparable to the cross-cell variability of the population 1−α1-\alpha estimate.

The §2.3 stimulus-frame caveat applies here: in real fixRSVP adjacent analysis bins inside a single 2020 Hz stimulus-frame interval share the same field draw, so the effective TT for (A6.1) is #fixations×#stim-frames per fixation\#\text{fixations}\times\#\text{stim-frames per fixation} rather than the raw bin count. With a 5050 ms stimulus-frame period and ∼6\sim 6–1212 post-fix bins per fixation (depending on bin width), the effective TT is reduced by the bins-per-frame multiplicity and the floor correspondingly rises. We do not propagate this correction through the real-data numbers in §4.5 — those are robust to it at the population median (Δ 1−α=−0.0221-\alpha = -0.022) — but it matters for per-cell SEM and is the operative reason the within-stimulus-frame reliability question is flagged as a future direction (§2.3).

A.9.3 Boundary-clipping bias

estimators.py:252 clips α̂\hat\alpha to [0,1][0,1] before reporting 1−α̂1-\hat\alpha. The clip is sensible — α\alpha is a fraction and the ratio of two variance estimates can excursion outside — but it introduces a bias on the mean whenever the sampling SD σα\sigma_\alpha is comparable to the distance from α*\alpha^* to the nearest boundary.

Treating α̂∼𝒩(α*,σα2)\hat\alpha\sim\mathcal N(\alpha^*, \sigma_\alpha^2) and clipping to [0,1][0,1], the post-clip mean is the truncated-Gaussian moment

𝔼[clip(α̂)]=α*[Φ(z1)−Φ(z0)]+σα[φ(z0)−φ(z1)]+[1−Φ(z1)], \mathbb E\!\left[\mathrm{clip}(\hat\alpha)\right] = \alpha^*\bigl[\Phi(z_1)-\Phi(z_0)\bigr] \;+\; \sigma_\alpha\bigl[\varphi(z_0)-\varphi(z_1)\bigr] \;+\; \bigl[1-\Phi(z_1)\bigr],

(A6.3)

with z0=−α*/σαz_0 = -\alpha^*/\sigma_\alpha, z1=(1−α*)/σαz_1 = (1-\alpha^*)/\sigma_\alpha, and φ,Φ\varphi,\Phi the standard normal pdf/cdf. The sign of the bias on 1−α̂1-\hat\alpha is set by which boundary is closer:

  • Small ℓ\ell (α*→0\alpha^*\to 0, 1−α*→11-\alpha^*\to 1): the lower tail of α̂\hat\alpha is clipped up to 00, so 𝔼[clip(α̂)]>α*\mathbb E[\mathrm{clip}(\hat\alpha)]>\alpha^* and 1−α̂1-\hat\alpha is biased down.
  • Large ℓ\ell (α*→1\alpha^*\to 1, 1−α*→01-\alpha^*\to 0): the upper tail of α̂\hat\alpha is clipped down to 11, so 𝔼[clip(α̂)]<α*\mathbb E[\mathrm{clip}(\hat\alpha)]<\alpha^* and 1−α̂1-\hat\alpha is biased up.

Both pull 1−α̂1-\hat\alpha toward the interior 1/21/2. Fig. A6B verifies (A6.3) on the small-ℓ\ell side: at ℓ=0.3σe\ell=0.3\sigma_e (α*=0.043\alpha^*=0.043), each (N,T)(N,T) cell produces an (sd,bias)(\mathrm{sd},\mathrm{bias}) pair, and the empirical points sit on the analytical curve as σα\sigma_\alpha is varied. The bias reaches ∼−0.08\sim -0.08 at σα≈0.3\sigma_\alpha\approx 0.3 — small in absolute terms, but a 9%9\% shift relative to the true 1−α*=0.9571-\alpha^*=0.957. The same effect explains the saturation of 1−α̂1-\hat\alpha at 00 for eccentric-mask cells (§4.1, Fig. 3), where the unclipped close-pair α̂\hat\alpha excursions above 11 from the inflated-CrateC_\text{rate} side of the naive inconsistency.

A.10 The PSTH covariance estimator: McFarland M6 vs bagged split-half

The §1 estimators of CpsthC_{\text{psth}} and CrateC_{\text{rate}} both rest on the cross-trial trick of (4) — distinct-trial products at the same time bin have independent observation noise, so the average product converges to the underlying rate product with the noise removed. The close-pair estimator (8) is one specific arrangement of this trick (pairs restricted to Δe<ε\Delta e<\varepsilon). The PSTH-covariance estimator (6) is another (pairs unrestricted on Δe\Delta e). Two computationally distinct estimators target (6); this appendix walks through why the naive PSTH covariance fails, names the two distinct-trial estimators that fix it, and records the choice this writeup makes.

A.10.1 Why the naive PSTH covariance is biased — and worse on off-diagonals

The textbook estimator of CpsthC_{\text{psth}} is the across-time-bin covariance of the per-bin trial-mean count

μ̂m(t)=1nt∑i=1ntYmi(t)=𝔼[Ym∣t]+1nt∑iξmi(t)⏟trial-mean noise, \hat\mu_m(t) \;=\; \frac{1}{n_t}\sum_{i=1}^{n_t} Y_m^i(t) \;=\; \mathbb E[Y_m\mid t] \;+\; \underbrace{\frac{1}{n_t}\sum_i \xi_m^i(t)}_{\text{trial-mean noise}},

where ξmi(t)=Ymi(t)−𝔼[Ym∣t]\xi_m^i(t) = Y_m^i(t) - \mathbb E[Y_m\mid t] collects every source of trial-to-trial variability at fixed time bin tt: single-cell Poisson, the FEM modulation rm(t,e)−𝔼e[rm∣t]r_m(t,e)-\mathbb E_e[r_m\mid t], and any shared latent that couples cells on the same trial. Taking Covt(μ̂m,μ̂n)\mathrm{Cov}_t(\hat\mu_m, \hat\mu_n) gives, in expectation,

𝔼[Covt̂(μ̂m,μ̂n)]=Covt(𝔼[Ym∣t],𝔼[Yn∣t])+𝔼t[Cov(ξm,ξn∣t)nt]. \mathbb E\!\big[\widehat{\mathrm{Cov}_t}(\hat\mu_m,\hat\mu_n)\big] \;=\; \mathrm{Cov}_t\!\big(\mathbb E[Y_m\mid t],\, \mathbb E[Y_n\mid t]\big) \;+\; \mathbb E_t\!\left[\frac{\mathrm{Cov}\!\big(\xi_m,\xi_n\mid t\big)}{n_t}\right].

(A7.1)

The first term is the PSTH covariance we want. The second term is a finite-trial contamination by the within-bin same-trial noise covariance, attenuated by 1/nt1/n_t but not gone. It has two distinct failure modes:

  • On the diagonal (m=n)(m=n), the second term is 𝔼t[Var(ξm∣t)/nt]\mathbb E_t[\mathrm{Var}(\xi_m\mid t)/n_t] — the familiar finite-trial inflation of any sample variance. McFarland and the older literature (Sahani–Linden 2003) routinely debias it via a within-bin variance subtraction −MSwithin/n0- MS_{\text{within}}/n_0.
  • On the off-diagonal (m≠n)(m\neq n) the second term is 𝔼t[Cov(ξm,ξn∣t)/nt]\mathbb E_t[\mathrm{Cov}(\xi_m,\xi_n\mid t)/n_t] — the simultaneous cross-cell noise correlation, attenuated by 1/nt1/n_t but present in expectation.
